stem: Let \( f(x, y) = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + ([[c]])xy + ([[d]]) \). Using the linear approximation (total differential) of \( f \) based at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \), estimate \( f([[x0]] + ([[dx]]),\ [[y0]] + ([[dy]])) \).
solution: The linear approximation is \( f([[x0]], [[y0]]) + f_{x}\,\Delta x + f_{y}\,\Delta y \), with \( f_{x} = 2([[a]])x + ([[c]])y \) and \( f_{y} = 2([[b]])y + ([[c]])x \) evaluated at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \), and \( \Delta x = [[dx]] \), \( \Delta y = [[dy]] \). This gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the exact value \( f([[x0]] + [[dx]], [[y0]] + [[dy]]) \), not the linear estimate.
This is only the differential \( df \); it forgets to add the base value \( f([[x0]], [[y0]]) \).
This subtracts the differential instead of adding it.
This swaps \( \Delta x \) and \( \Delta y \) in the differential.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x, y) = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + ([[c]])xy + ([[d]]) \). As \( (x, y) \) moves from \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) to \( ([[x0]] + ([[dx]]),\ [[y0]] + ([[dy]])) \), use the total differential \( df = f_{x}\,dx + f_{y}\,dy \) to estimate the resulting CHANGE in \( f \) (not the new function value).
solution: The total differential is \( df = f_{x}\,dx + f_{y}\,dy \), with \( f_{x} = 2([[a]])x + ([[c]])y \) and \( f_{y} = 2([[b]])y + ([[c]])x \) evaluated at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \). Substituting \( dx=[[dx]] \), \( dy=[[dy]] \) gives \( df = [[answer]] \), the estimated change in \( f \).
distractor_rationales: This is the estimated NEW function value \( f([[x0]]+[[dx]], [[y0]]+[[dy]]) \), not just the change \( df \).
This is the EXACT change in \( f \) (computed from the actual function values), not the linear-approximation estimate \( df \).
This swaps \( dx \) and \( dy \) in the differential.
This uses only the \( x \)-term of the differential and drops the \( y \)-term.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: A rectangular box has dimensions \( x = [[x0]] \), \( y = [[y0]] \), \( z = [[z0]] \) (in cm), each measured with a small error. Its volume is \( V = xyz \). If the measurement errors are \( dx = [[dx]] \), \( dy = [[dy]] \), \( dz = [[dz]] \) (in cm), use the total differential of \( V \) to estimate the resulting change in volume \( dV \).
solution: The total differential of \( V = xyz \) is \( dV = V_{x}\,dx + V_{y}\,dy + V_{z}\,dz = yz\,dx + xz\,dy + xy\,dz \). Substituting the given dimensions and errors gives \( dV = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the EXACT change in volume, not the linear-approximation estimate \( dV \).
This keeps only the \( dx \)-term of the differential and drops the other two.
This multiplies the three errors together instead of forming the weighted sum \( yz\,dx+xz\,dy+xy\,dz \).
This cyclically shifts which pairwise product multiplies which error.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: A quantity is modeled by \( Q = x^{[[m]]}y^{[[n]]} \). Measurements give \( x = [[x0]] \), \( y = [[y0]] \), with small measurement errors \( dx = [[dx]] \) and \( dy = [[dy]] \). Using the total differential of \( \ln Q = [[m]]\ln x + [[n]]\ln y \), estimate the RELATIVE error \( \dfrac{dQ}{Q} \) (not the absolute error \( dQ \)).
solution: Taking logarithms, \( \ln Q = [[m]]\ln x + [[n]]\ln y \), so differentiating gives \( \dfrac{dQ}{Q} = [[m]]\dfrac{dx}{x} + [[n]]\dfrac{dy}{y} \). Substituting the given values gives \( \dfrac{dQ}{Q} = [[m]]\cdot\dfrac{[[dx]]}{[[x0]]} + [[n]]\cdot\dfrac{[[dy]]}{[[y0]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide each error by its base value, using the absolute differential formula instead.
This drops the exponents \( [[m]] \) and \( [[n]] \) entirely.
This swaps which exponent multiplies which relative error.
This subtracts the second term instead of adding it.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x, y) = \sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}} \), the distance from the origin. Using the linear approximation of \( f \) based at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) (where \( f([[x0]],[[y0]]) = [[r0]] \)), estimate \( f([[x0]] + ([[dx]]),\ [[y0]] + ([[dy]])) \).
distractor_rationales: This is the EXACT distance to the nearby point, not the linear-approximation estimate.
This treats both partial derivatives as exactly 1, dropping the \( x_{0}/r_{0} \) and \( y_{0}/r_{0} \) factors.
This is only the differential \( df \); it forgets to add the base value \( f([[x0]],[[y0]]) = [[r0]] \).
This swaps \( f_{x} \) and \( f_{y} \).
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( T(x, y, z) = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + [[c]]z^{2} \) (three independent variables). Using the total differential \( dT = T_{x}\,dx + T_{y}\,dy + T_{z}\,dz \), estimate the change in \( T \) as \( (x,y,z) \) moves from \( ([[x0]], [[y0]], [[z0]]) \) by \( dx=[[dx]] \), \( dy=[[dy]] \), \( dz=[[dz]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the factor of 2 that comes from differentiating each squared term.
This forgets the \( z \)-term of the differential entirely, as if \( T \) only depended on \( x \) and \( y \).
This is the EXACT change in \( T \), not the linear-approximation estimate \( dT \).
This evaluates each partial derivative at the base point but forgets to multiply by the corresponding coordinate value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x, y) = [[a]]x^{2} + ([[b]])xy + [[c]]y^{2} \). A measurement of \( (x, y) \) at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) has error bounds \( |dx| \le [[ex]] \) and \( |dy| \le [[ey]] \). Using \( |df| \le |f_{x}|\,|dx| + |f_{y}|\,|dy| \), what is the maximum possible estimated error in \( f \)?
solution: The partials are \( f_{x} = 2([[a]])x+([[b]])y \) and \( f_{y} = ([[b]])x+2([[c]])y \), evaluated at \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \). The worst-case (maximum) error bound is \( |f_{x}|\,[[ex]] + |f_{y}|\,[[ey]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the absolute value bars, allowing signs to cancel instead of taking the worst case.
This swaps which error bound multiplies which partial derivative.
This forgets to multiply each partial derivative's magnitude by its corresponding error bound.
This subtracts the second term instead of adding it, understating the worst-case error.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
